title: Which states and products risk the greatest losses if the USMCA is terminated?
gist: PIIE economists size up the exposure of US states and product categories to a low-probability but non-zero USMCA termination, in which the US could impose uniform 10-15% tariffs on Canada/Mexico imports and trigger retaliation. The most exposed exporters are autos/parts, petroleum, computers, aircraft, and semiconductors, concentrated in states (Texas, Michigan, Indiana) that mostly voted Trump in 2024.

insights.claim: PIIE judges outright USMCA termination during the current 6-year review highly unlikely but explicitly not zero-probability, because Trump is making heavy demands on Canada and Mexico while offering no concessions on US trade barriers.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: A tail risk worth hedging: North American supply-chain names face episodic tariff-headline volatility even if base case is no termination.
insights.quote: But the risk is not zero for the simple reason that Trump is making heavy demands on Canada and Mexico while offering no concessions on US trade barriers.

insights.claim: If USMCA simply lapses with no agreement, it triggers annual reviews for the next 10 years, removing business certainty even without formal termination.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: A 'soft' negative outcome that markets may underprice: prolonged uncertainty discourages North American capex without a single headline trigger.
insights.quote: the prospect of annual reviews would create a cloud over North American trade and investment and deprive the USMCA of a key attribute: business certainty
insights.locator: 'First, the review could result in no agreement'

insights.claim: Mere termination notice would resurrect MFN tariffs averaging ~3% (US), ~6% (Canada), and ~7% (Mexico) between the partners.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: Quantifies the baseline tariff floor on cross-border trade if the pact ends; modest on average but uneven by product.
insights.quote: US MFN tariffs average around 3 percent, Canadian MFN tariffs around 6 percent, and Mexican around 7 percent.

insights.claim: A worst-case termination could see the US impose uniform 10-15% tariffs on Canada/Mexico imports plus national-security tariffs of 50% on iron/steel and 25% on the non-North American component of autos, with selective retaliation.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: Defines the stress scenario for North American autos and metals; the 25% non-NA-content auto tariff specifically penalizes globalized supply chains.
insights.quote: the US administration might impose uniform tariffs of 10 to 15 percent on imports from Canada and Mexico, along with higher tariffs for national security claims (e.g., 50 percent on iron and steel, 25 percent on the non–North American component of autos)

insights.claim: Texas exports $159.8 billion to Canada and Mexico (35.5% of its exports), and the Southwest region alone ships $147.4 billion to Mexico, making Texas/Southwest the single largest geographic exposure to USMCA disruption.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: Texas-heavy exporters (energy, tech, aerospace) carry the most absolute downside in a termination scenario.

insights.claim: North Dakota sends 89.9% of its total exports to Canada and Mexico and Michigan 64.9%, making these states the most concentrated and politically combustible in a termination fight.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: High-concentration states are most likely to lobby against termination, a political check that limits the tail risk.

insights.claim: Seven of the nine states with per-capita exports to Canada/Mexico over $2,000 voted for Trump in 2024, creating internal political 'blowback' that should deter the administration from threatening termination.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: The political economy itself is the main brake on termination risk; watch red-state congressional pushback as the live signal.
insights.quote: It is worth noting that seven of the nine high-impact states voted for Trump in 2024.
insights.locator: 'Table 2 shows states with per capita exports'

insights.claim: Auto parts (HS 8708) send 75.6% and goods-transport vehicles (HS 8704) 77.7% of all US exports to Canada and Mexico, making automotive the most concentrated category and an easy retaliation target.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: US auto-parts and truck makers have the least ability to diversify away from North America; first-order retaliation casualties.
insights.quote: These would be easy targets for higher Canadian and Mexican tariffs if the United States ramps up protection against their auto exports.

insights.claim: Texas exported roughly $42.6 billion of petroleum products to Canada and Mexico in 2025, the single largest product-state exposure cited.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: Refined-product exporters and Gulf Coast refiners face the largest dollar exposure to Mexican/Canadian retaliatory tariffs.
insights.quote: Texas, for example, exported roughly $42.6 billion of petroleum products to Canada and Mexico in 2025.

insights.claim: Texas, California, Arizona, and New Mexico together exported ~$11 billion of semiconductors to Canada and Mexico in 2025, including GPUs and CPUs from NVIDIA, Intel, and AMD.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: Names specific chip exposure to North American retaliation, relevant for semiconductor revenue routing through Mexican/Canadian assembly.
insights.quote: This includes processors such as graphics processing units (GPUs) and central processing units (CPUs), including products sold by technology firms such as NVIDIA, Intel, and AMD.
insights.locator: 'Texas, California, Arizona, and New Mexico together contributed to some $11 billion of exports of semiconductors'

insights.claim: About $33 billion of data-processing machines (HS 8471/8473) flow from Texas, Arizona, New Mexico, and California to Canada/Mexico, including products from Apple, Dell, and HP, while ~$11 billion of civilian aircraft involves Boeing.
insights.speaker: Gary Clyde Hufbauer,Ye Zhang
insights.so_what: Hardware and aerospace OEMs have material North American export dependence that could shift to domestic or third-country suppliers under retaliation.
insights.locator: 'Texas, Arizona, New Mexico, and California together contributed to some $33 billion'
